findings.specific_passage: 'if you were independent, we'd consider you' is almost exactly zero in actual conversion terms. It means they like you and they're being polite. The number of those conversations that convert to signed engagements in the first 3 months is typically less than 1 in 5, and often zero.
findings.challenge_statement: What evidence here is actually representative, and what is merely vivid, recent, or easy to recall?
findings.next_move: Write the disconfirming evidence that would outweigh the salient anecdote before moving forward.
major_tensions: Salience correction is fake if the team mentions a denominator but still lets the memorable anecdote decide the case.
findings.intervention_hint: Write the disconfirming evidence that would outweigh the salient anecdote before moving forward.

findings.specific_passage: Launching in 6 weeks is viable if you do three things in those 6 weeks: (1) have the full runway conversation with your spouse, (2) go back to your network with the specific fractional ask and see if 1-2 convert, (3) prepare for months 1-3 as business development, not delivery. If after 4 weeks you have zero fractional commitments and your spouse is not aligned on the 5-months-of-revenue-pressure reality, push back by 2-3 months.
findings.challenge_statement: If the team were not already living inside this setup, what fresh evidence would justify choosing it again today?
findings.next_move: Separate real switching cost from habit comfort, then test one concrete alternative before renewing the incumbent path.
major_tensions: Status-quo correction is fake when the review lists switching pain but never asks whether the current arrangement still earns renewal.
findings.intervention_hint: Separate real switching cost from habit comfort, then test one concrete alternative before renewing the incumbent path.

chunks.text: A premortem becomes superficial when it produces an obvious worry list without owners, mitigations, or decision changes.

This model depends on counterfactual-reasoning (applied-form-of), which is not active in the answer. Premortem IS applied counterfactual reasoning. The core exercise — imagine the project failed, then explain why — is a counterfactual scenario by definition. Without the capacity for counterfactual thought, premortem cannot function.
Do we still remember the question we are trying to answer, or have we substituted an easier one?
Commitment Bias: Commitment-bias hardens public commitment into escalation, making it harder for a premortem to bind reversal triggers before more sunk cost accumulates.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Strong specific mechanism — publicly stated positions harden into escalation-of-commitment, making genuine failure exploration feel like disloyalty precisely when backward analysis is most needed.
chunks.activation_condition: When the team has already publicly committed to the plan and sunk cost makes reversing feel psychologically costly

chunks.text: Assume the project failed and work backward from that future state to surface the weak links that could have caused it.

anchors.evidence_quote: If you don't have an engagement by month 5, your remaining 3 months of runway pressure-cooks you into taking whatever comes along, which is usually badly-fit work.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer anticipates pre-commitment failure modes like runway depletion leading to suboptimal decisions and uses them to shape the launch criteria.

chunks.text: Select when: Outcomes should shape the next action and the system can observe consequences quickly enough to learn instead of repeating the same move blindly. | Danger when: The organization is collecting signal but never translating it into changed behavior, priorities, or design. | Reasoning: causal, systems
Impulsivity can push the team into action before enough feedback has been gathered to understand the consequences.
What core standards have we failed to meet in the past, and how are we ensuring those same metrics remain absolute this quarter?
Run the goal-problem-diagnose-design-do cycle continuously so results feed back into the next round of action.
Refine the next move iteratively based on what the last output revealed instead of restarting from scratch.

anchors.evidence_quote: If after 4 weeks you have zero fractional commitments and your spouse is not aligned on the 5-months-of-revenue-pressure reality, push back by 2-3 months. That's not failure; that's responding to signal.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer establishes a feedback loop where early experimental outcomes directly trigger plan adjustments before full commitment.

chunks.text: Over-reliance on the mean can hide low-probability, high-impact outcomes that matter more than the base case.
Availability and representativeness can distort probability estimates by privileging vivid stories over statistical fit.
Frame decisions as expected-value calculations instead of looking for options with no downside at all.
What data is missing that might spoil our story?
Confirmation Bias: Confirmation-bias corrupts probabilistic-thinking by treating belief defense as if it were evidence weighting.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'This bias represents a failure of critical thinking, where people seek information that confirms existing beliefs. PT requires intellectual humility to identify disconfirming evidence.'
chunks.activation_condition: When existing beliefs distort probability estimates by selectively seeking confirming rather than disconfirming evidence.

anchors.evidence_quote: The number of those conversations that convert to signed engagements in the first 3 months is typically less than 1 in 5, and often zero.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer uses probabilistic estimates of conversion rates to frame the pipeline strength as less-likely rather than binary yes-or-no.

chunks.text: Decision makers can confuse similarity with probability and treat a plausible story as if it were a strong forecast.
Force an outside-view pass with reference classes or base rates before acting on an intuitive category match.

This model depends on system-2 (requires), which is not active in the answer. The representativeness heuristic is a System 1 shortcut. Detecting and overriding it requires System 2 engagement to pause intuitive categorization and force comparison against base rates, alternative classes, or regression effects.
If this representative-looking pattern fails, what ignored denominator or low-probability scenario would explain the miss?
Is the worst case bad enough if this resemblance-based judgment is wrong?

The number of those conversations that convert to signed engagements in the first 3 months is typically less than 1 in 5, and often zero.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer corrects a judgment driven by surface similarity to polite interest by emphasizing actual base rates and conversion frequencies rather than vivid category matches.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Base-rates directly counter representativeness-heuristic by forcing denominator discipline when vivid resemblance is doing the work of judgment.
expansions.why_relevant: Base-rates directly counter representativeness-heuristic by forcing denominator discipline when vivid resemblance is doing the work of judgment.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'RH, by definition, ignores critical components of rational probability, such as the base rate and statistical laws' — definitional opposition.
expansions.activation_condition: When resemblance-based categorization overrides the actual statistical frequency of events in the reference class.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Representativeness-heuristic conflicts with base-rates when vivid similarity overrides reference-class frequency and outside-view distribution evidence.
expansions.why_relevant: Representativeness-heuristic conflicts with base-rates when vivid similarity overrides reference-class frequency and outside-view distribution evidence.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Bayesian updating resists representativeness-heuristic by revising on evidence instead of trusting intuitive category fit.
expansions.why_relevant: Bayesian updating resists representativeness-heuristic by revising on evidence instead of trusting intuitive category fit.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Bayesian reasoning requires incorporating prior probabilities and updating with evidence — directly corrects the representativeness heuristic's neglect of base rates.
expansions.activation_condition: When proper Bayesian updating would correct the intuitive probability estimate distorted by surface similarity.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias corrupts probabilistic-thinking by treating belief defense as if it were evidence weighting.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias corrupts probabilistic-thinking by treating belief defense as if it were evidence weighting.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'This bias represents a failure of critical thinking, where people seek information that confirms existing beliefs. PT requires intellectual humility to identify disconfirming evidence.'
expansions.activation_condition: When existing beliefs distort probability estimates by selectively seeking confirming rather than disconfirming evidence.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Probabilistic-thinking conflicts with narratives when story coherence makes a scenario feel more probable than its base rate warrants, overriding distributional reasoning.
expansions.why_relevant: Probabilistic-thinking conflicts with narratives when story coherence makes a scenario feel more probable than its base rate warrants, overriding distributional reasoning.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Representativeness-bias can make probabilistic-thinking judge likelihood by resemblance to a familiar story instead of by the actual odds.
expansions.why_relevant: Representativeness-bias can make probabilistic-thinking judge likelihood by resemblance to a familiar story instead of by the actual odds.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'probabilities are incorrectly judged based on resemblance to a stereotype rather than mathematical likelihood' — System 1 substitution that defeats probabilistic reasoning.
expansions.activation_condition: When resemblance-based intuitions override proper base-rate calculations and statistical reasoning.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Commitment-bias hardens public commitment into escalation, making it harder for a premortem to bind reversal triggers before more sunk cost accumulates.
expansions.why_relevant: Commitment-bias hardens public commitment into escalation, making it harder for a premortem to bind reversal triggers before more sunk cost accumulates.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Strong specific mechanism — publicly stated positions harden into escalation-of-commitment, making genuine failure exploration feel like disloyalty precisely when backward analysis is most needed.
expansions.activation_condition: When the team has already publicly committed to the plan and sunk cost makes reversing feel psychologically costly

expansions.substrate_chunk: Premortem conflicts with commitment-bias when publicly stated positions harden into escalation-of-commitment and teams reinterpret disconfirming signals as temporary noise.
expansions.why_relevant: Premortem conflicts with commitment-bias when publicly stated positions harden into escalation-of-commitment and teams reinterpret disconfirming signals as temporary noise.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias makes premortem ceremonial by defending the favored plan instead of searching for failure causes that could really break it.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias makes premortem ceremonial by defending the favored plan instead of searching for failure causes that could really break it.
expansions.affinity_rationale: The premortem was explicitly designed to override confirmation bias — described as 'an effective technique for overriding' it — making confirmation bias the primary threat premortem exists to counter.
expansions.activation_condition: When teams use premortem exercises but keep defending the favored plan instead of genuinely searching for failure causes that could break it

expansions.substrate_chunk: Feedback-loops conflicts with circle-of-control when the team optimizes only immediate controllables and misses how signals propagate through the wider system.
expansions.why_relevant: Feedback-loops conflicts with circle-of-control when the team optimizes only immediate controllables and misses how signals propagate through the wider system.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias blocks effective feedback loops by resisting the evidence that should change the next action.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias blocks effective feedback loops by resisting the evidence that should change the next action.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Described as 'resistance to disconfirming evidence' that acts as 'model-defending behavior' — directly blocks the acknowledgment and learning from mistakes that effective feedback loops require.
expansions.activation_condition: When feedback signals contain disconfirming evidence that the recipient is psychologically wired to dismiss in favor of existing beliefs

expansions.substrate_chunk: Feedback-loops conflicts with optionality when constant option switching prevents stable interpretation of signal quality over time.
expansions.why_relevant: Feedback-loops conflicts with optionality when constant option switching prevents stable interpretation of signal quality over time.

failure_hints.text: A vivid pattern match can crowd out base-rate and frequency discipline, making the case feel more diagnostic than it is.
Decision makers can confuse similarity with probability and treat a plausible story as if it were a strong forecast.
Availability and representativeness can distort probability estimates by privileging vivid stories over statistical fit.
Over-reliance on the mean can hide low-probability, high-impact outcomes that matter more than the base case.
A premortem becomes superficial when it produces an obvious worry list without owners, mitigations, or decision changes.
Mental fatigue can make the exercise lazy, obvious, and non-imaginative because participants default to fast thinking instead of deeper failure exploration.
A system can drift to low performance when bad results quietly reset the standard instead of triggering correction.
Impulsivity can push the team into action before enough feedback has been gathered to understand the consequences.

heuristic_hints.text: Force an outside-view pass with reference classes or base rates before acting on an intuitive category match.
Challenge the favored frame with thesis, antithesis, and synthesis instead of letting the first pattern match become the decision.
Decorrelate errors by collecting independent probability judgments before group discussion starts.
Frame decisions as expected-value calculations instead of looking for options with no downside at all.
Assume the project failed and work backward from that future state to surface the weak links that could have caused it.
Expose second-order consequences and domino effects instead of evaluating only the first visible step.
Refine the next move iteratively based on what the last output revealed instead of restarting from scratch.
Run the goal-problem-diagnose-design-do cycle continuously so results feed back into the next round of action.

premortem_hints.text: If this representative-looking pattern fails, what ignored denominator or low-probability scenario would explain the miss?
Is the worst case bad enough if this resemblance-based judgment is wrong?
Is the worst case bad enough?
What data is missing that might spoil our story?
Do we still remember the question we are trying to answer, or have we substituted an easier one?
Is the worst case bad enough?
Are we mistreating this system because we are unaware of the delays, limiting factors, or false boundaries influencing the results?
What core standards have we failed to meet in the past, and how are we ensuring those same metrics remain absolute this quarter?

select_when: A judgment is being driven by surface similarity or a vivid category match rather than by rates, frequencies, or causal detail.
People are substituting the question 'What does this resemble?' for the harder question 'What is statistically or causally likely here?'
The risk is that a compelling pattern match will crowd out denominator discipline.
A decision maker is intuitively classifying a case before checking whether the reference class actually supports that classification.
A vivid story, category label, or familiar prototype is overpowering statistical reasoning in a forecast or judgment.
A recommendation depends on forcing a handoff from intuitive categorization to explicit outside-view checking before action.
Problem solvers are at risk of declaring that they have seen this pattern before and skipping verification of whether the old frame still fits.
danger_when: The model would be used as a generic anti-intuition slogan instead of checking where resemblance is actually informative.
Teams are treating every pattern match as wrong even when there is strong base-rate and causal support for it.
The judgment already rests on explicit outside-view evidence and the model would only restate a bias warning without changing the decision path.
The model is being used to criticize simplification itself rather than the specific move from surface similarity to unsupported causal confidence.

select_when: The decision is not yes-or-no but more-likely-or-less-likely, so binary framing would hide meaningful uncertainty.
The team needs to compare ranges, distributions, or expected value instead of treating one forecast as certain.
Uncertainty can be reduced through better priors, disconfirming evidence, or explicit probability ranges rather than by telling a cleaner story.
The current argument sounds precise, but the real task is to quantify how much confidence the team actually has.
danger_when: The work requires a concrete commitment now, but the team is using probability language to postpone choosing after the key economics are already clear.
The probability estimates are being treated as exact-looking numbers without enough evidence, base-rate discipline, or sensitivity testing behind them.

select_when: A project is still pre-commitment and the cost of finding failure modes now is much lower than after launch.
Social pressure, optimism, or executive momentum would otherwise suppress dissent and make the plan feel safer than it is.
Success depends on multiple conditions lining up, so the team needs to expose weak links, reversibility limits, and contingency gaps in advance.
The main risk is not effort but unwarranted optimism about a conjunctive plan with too many silent assumptions.
danger_when: The exercise is producing an obvious list of worries without owners, mitigations, or decision changes tied to what was surfaced.
The team is using premortem language performatively while remaining unwilling to challenge the favored plan or change commitments.

select_when: Outcomes should shape the next action and the system can observe consequences quickly enough to learn instead of repeating the same move blindly.
Behavior changes the environment and the environment changes behavior back, so the problem depends on circular rather than one-way causality.
Measurement can actually close the loop because signals are tied to real levers and the next adjustment can follow from what the system just produced.
The work happens inside a complex dynamic system where delays, adaptation, and iteration matter more than a static snapshot.
danger_when: The organization is collecting signal but never translating it into changed behavior, priorities, or design.
The loop is being treated as progress even though the team is stuck in endless questioning or one-way communication that never closes into action.

frame_elements.element_text: informal network conversations constitute a viable pipeline for launch
frame_elements.evidence_quote: Pipeline — I've had informal conversations with 4-5 former colleagues and people in my network who've said things like "if you were independent, we'd consider you."
frame_elements.fragility_signal: industry conversion rates show <20% turn into signed engagements

frame_elements.element_text: launch must occur in exactly 6 weeks
frame_elements.evidence_quote: Plan is to go independent starting in 6 weeks.
frame_elements.fragility_signal: better to launch with signed LOIs regardless of calendar timing

frame_elements.element_text: 8 months zero revenue runway is sufficient without needing first engagement sooner
frame_elements.evidence_quote: Runway — 8 months assumes zero revenue. I figured if I can't get my first engagement in 8 months, something is fundamentally wrong.
frame_elements.fragility_signal: first engagements typically take 3-5 months, leaving only 3 months buffer

reframings.reframed_question: Of the 4-5 former colleagues who've expressed interest in your independent services, which 2 are most likely to sign an engagement in the next 3 months, and what specific actions would convert their interest to commitment??
reframings.what_opens: Prioritizes and tests the informal network pipeline by focusing on high-potential leads and conversion steps, revealing true viability beyond vague interest.

reframings.reframed_question: If your first B2B fintech engagement typically takes 3-5 months to close as per industry norms, how would you adjust your launch plan to secure it within the first 3 months of your 8-month runway??
reframings.what_opens: Incorporates realistic engagement timelines to stress-test runway adequacy, prompting accelerated pipeline actions for buffer preservation.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Distinguishes sunk costs (past network convos) from future costs; identifies switching barriers (employer noticing BD, financial pressure); recommends staging commitment with 4-week checkpoint to preserve optionality of delaying by 2-3 months.

Explicitly weighs 6-week launch vs delay by 2-3 months; rationale for sequence (spouse convo + fractional asks first, then assess); critical path (fundamentals before tactics); delay impact (better pipeline conversion vs employer risk).

Identifies spouse as key stakeholder (supportive of concept but needs specifics); influence strategy (have full runway convo this week); distinguishes technical plan from feasibility (spouse alignment required); failure mode (fights over money).

Acknowledges 8-month runway tightness and revenue ramp but does not identify displaced alternatives (e.g., staying employed longer, other career paths) or compare marginal returns of launch vs delay/no-launch.
dimensions.materiality_note: High materiality: Reallocating time/resources to current job or alternatives could extend runway indefinitely, reversing launch recommendation.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Names risks (zero conversions, pressure-cooking into bad work) but does not size downside exposure (e.g., probability/bounds of scenarios), distinguish mitigate vs adapt strategies, or detail recovery paths beyond delay.
dimensions.materiality_note: High materiality: Different risk posture (e.g., more mitigation via fractional safety net quantification) could favor immediate launch or full delay.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Mentions network politeness vs real intent but does not analyze principal-agent gaps (e.g., client incentives to commit) or alignment mechanisms (monitoring, contracts) for fractional/project conversions.
dimensions.materiality_note: Medium materiality: Misaligned client incentives could explain low conversion, suggesting stronger pre-commitment mechanisms and altering launch viability.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Cites 'industry experience' of <1/5 conversion and 3-5 month ramps as fact without assessing source reliability, sample bias, or confounders; no base rates or missing evidence check.
dimensions.materiality_note: Immaterial: User's specific network/experience may differ; answer's directional realism holds without precise calibration.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Treats conversion rates as estimable risk without classifying epistemic (reducible via tests) vs Knightian; no calibration of confidence or experiments to reduce (beyond asks).
dimensions.materiality_note: Immaterial: Conditional plan (test in 4 weeks) implicitly addresses reducible uncertainty.

questions: What other personal or financial commitments compete with the 8-month zero-revenue runway for your resources?
If you reallocated time or money from the 6-week launch prep to pipeline building, what would that look like in practice?
What resources are currently abundant enough to support launching without signed commitments?
What specific risks worry you most about launching in 6 weeks with no committed pipeline?
How have you adapted to similar business development risks in past career transitions?
What mitigation steps beyond spouse talks and network asks are you already planning for the 8-month runway?
What incentives does your spouse have to support sticking to the 8-month runway under revenue pressure?
Who else in your network might act as an accountability partner for pipeline conversion before launch?
How would you structure monitoring of your fractional commitments to align your own incentives during months 1-3?
What evidence from your 4-5 network conversations signals real demand beyond polite interest?
What additional data on fintech consulting conversion rates from informal talks to signed deals do you have access to?
How reliable is your 8-month runway estimate given past personal spending patterns?
Which part of the launch feels like predictable risk (e.g., pipeline conversion) versus true unknown (e.g., market demand)?
What ignorance gaps about B2B fintech clients post-launch could you close in the next 4 weeks?
How much of the 6-week timeline decision stems from measurable runway math versus unquantifiable spouse dynamics?

triage_scores.evidence: Answer overweighted vivid 'industry experience' claim that 'first paid engagement often takes 3-5 months' plus conversion rate '<1 in 5' as sufficient denominator for tight runway judgment, without citing or checking broader base rates for first-time fintech B2B consultants.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Assess pipeline strength by distinguishing between informal interest and committed engagements
evidence_quotes: "if you were independent, we'd consider you" is almost exactly zero in actual conversion terms.
The number of those conversations that convert to signed engagements in the first 3 months is typically less than 1 in 5, and often zero.
You don't have a pipeline; you have a network that might become a pipeline.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes directly evaluate the user's 4-5 informal conversations as insufficient pipeline, using conversion statistics and terminology to downgrade from pipeline to network.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Evaluate runway adequacy by projecting realistic timelines for first revenue against zero-revenue duration
evidence_quotes: 8 months at zero revenue is tight for a first-time independent consultant.
Industry experience suggests the first paid engagement often takes 3-5 months from launch, not including the 1-2 months you'll spend setting up legal/admin.
"8 months runway" actually means "5 months of revenue pressure" because the first 3 months won't produce income.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes apply industry benchmarks to user's 8-month runway, calculating effective pressure period after setup and BD phases.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Identify spouse alignment gap between conceptual support and specific financial reality
evidence_quotes: Not "are you on board with independent consulting" — you already have that. It's "here's the specific scenario where I launch in September with no signed engagements, here's the burn rate, here's when we'd be under financial pressure, here's what we'd do."
Supportive-in-concept but not-aligned-on-specifics is exactly how couples end up fighting about money six months into a new business.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes contrast user's stated spouse support with need for detailed financial discussion, warning of risks from incomplete alignment.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Generate conditional launch viability criteria based on addressing key risks in the 6-week window
evidence_quotes: Launching in 6 weeks is viable if you do three things in those 6 weeks: (1) have the full runway conversation with your spouse, (2) go back to your network with the specific fractional ask and see if 1-2 convert, (3) prepare for months 1-3 as business development, not delivery.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quote explicitly lists three actionable prerequisites tying directly to identified risks (spouse, pipeline, expectations), making launch contingent on them.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Propose fractional role as de-risking bridge to extend effective runway while preserving independence
evidence_quotes: a part-time arrangement with a friendly ex-employer or fractional CTO/CPO role at one company that covers 30-40% of your baseline costs while the rest of the pipeline builds.
"Would your company be open to 10 hours/week at $12K/month for the next 6 months, starting in September?" is a very specific ask.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes define fractional option with specifics on hours, pay, targets, and benefits, positioning it as safety net for no commitments.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Establish decision trigger for delay based on empirical signals after partial execution period
evidence_quotes: If after 4 weeks you have zero fractional commitments and your spouse is not aligned on the 5-months-of-revenue-pressure reality, push back by 2-3 months.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quote sets measurable 4-week checkpoint with binary outcomes (commitments, alignment) leading to delay recommendation.

companion_fingerprint_validated.reasoning_move: Assess pipeline strength by distinguishing between informal interest and committed engagements
evidence_quotes: "if you were independent, we'd consider you" is almost exactly zero in actual conversion terms.
The number of those conversations that convert to signed engagements in the first 3 months is typically less than 1 in 5, and often zero.
You don't have a pipeline; you have a network that might become a pipeline.
companion_fingerprint_validated.evidence_rationale: Quotes directly evaluate the user's 4-5 informal conversations as insufficient pipeline, using conversion statistics and terminology to downgrade from pipeline to network.

"8 months runway" actually means "5 months of revenue pressure" because the first 3 months won't produce income.

companion_fingerprint_validated.reasoning_move: Identify spouse alignment gap between conceptual support and specific financial reality
evidence_quotes: Not "are you on board with independent consulting" — you already have that. It's "here's the specific scenario where I launch in September with no signed engagements, here's the burn rate, here's when we'd be under financial pressure, here's what we'd do."
Supportive-in-concept but not-aligned-on-specifics is exactly how couples end up fighting about money six months into a new business.
companion_fingerprint_validated.evidence_rationale: Quotes contrast user's stated spouse support with need for detailed financial discussion, warning of risks from incomplete alignment.

companion_fingerprint_validated.reasoning_move: Generate conditional launch viability criteria based on addressing key risks in the 6-week window

query:
Whether the user should launch independent B2B fintech consulting practice in 6 weeks or delay, given no committed pipeline and 8-month zero-revenue runway.

Constraints stated during conversation:
- [ACTIVE] Pipeline: 4-5 informal network conversations, no signed commitments
- [ACTIVE] Runway: 8 months at zero revenue
- [ACTIVE] Launch timeline: 6 weeks from now, aligned with Q3 planning end
- [ACTIVE] Spouse support: on board with concept, not specifics of financial pressure

Original framing: User seeks tactical launch plan for independent consulting starting in 6 weeks, assuming 8-month runway and network conversations as sufficient pipeline; excludes delaying launch or questioning premise.

Dropped threads (raised but unresolved):
- Tactical launch plan details (pricing, positioning, website, legal structure) (raised by user, status: acknowledged_then_dropped) → superseded by: focus on fundamentals like pipeline conversion, runway realism, spouse alignment, and fractional bridge
vanilla_answer:
SYNTHESIZED POSITION:
Launching in 6 weeks is viable if you do three things in those 6 weeks: (1) have the full runway conversation with your spouse, (2) go back to your network with the specific fractional ask and see if 1-2 convert, (3) prepare for months 1-3 as business development, not delivery. If after 4 weeks you have zero fractional commitments and your spouse is not aligned on the 5-months-of-revenue-pressure reality, push back by 2-3 months.

FULL ASSISTANT REASONING:
Happy to help with the launch plan. Before diving into tactics, can I ask a few things to make sure we're solving the right problem.

First: what's your current pipeline of consulting leads? Specifically, do you have prospective clients who've said "yes, once you're available, we want to engage you," or are you planning to build the pipeline during the transition?

Second: the 8 months runway — does that assume zero revenue, or is there an assumed ramp (like $0 for 2 months, then gradually increasing)? Those are different financial plans.

Third: the 6-week transition — why 6 weeks specifically? Is that notice period, last day aligned with something at your current job, or did you pick a number that felt right?

---

OK, useful context. I want to flag some things that are worth addressing before you give notice rather than after.

On pipeline: "if you were independent, we'd consider you" is almost exactly zero in actual conversion terms. It means they like you and they're being polite. The number of those conversations that convert to signed engagements in the first 3 months is typically less than 1 in 5, and often zero. You don't have a pipeline; you have a network that might become a pipeline.

On runway: 8 months at zero revenue is tight for a first-time independent consultant. Industry experience suggests the first paid engagement often takes 3-5 months from launch, not including the 1-2 months you'll spend setting up legal/admin. If you don't have an engagement by month 5, your remaining 3 months of runway pressure-cooks you into taking whatever comes along, which is usually badly-fit work.

On timing: Q3 planning cycle is a clean exit for your employer but I'd argue it's the wrong exit for you. A better exit timing would be when you have at least one signed LOI or verbal commitment for an engagement that starts within 30-60 days of your last day. Launching without that is possible but harder than launching with it.

---

You're right, I am. I'm doing that because the tactical advice — pricing, positioning, website, legal structure — only matters if the fundamentals are solid. Right now the fundamentals are shaky in a specific way that a lot of first-time independents don't see until they're 4 months in and burning through savings.

I can absolutely help with launch tactics. But if you want me to, I want you to at least acknowledge that the "network conversations aren't a pipeline" issue is real, so the launch plan we build accounts for it rather than assumes it away. Does that land?

---

Delay is one option but it's not the only one. Here are three real versions, with different tradeoffs.

Option 1: Delay launch by 2-3 months and spend that time converting network conversations into signed LOIs. This is the conservative path. You'd stay at your current job while explicitly telling your network "I'm going independent in September — is this conversation the start of an actual engagement?" The risk: your current employer may notice you're spending evening hours business-developing, which creates its own problems.

Option 2: Launch on your current timeline but accept that months 1-3 are business development, not delivery. Use the 8 months runway with that plan: months 1-3 are pipeline-building, months 4-6 are first engagements, months 7-8 are consolidation. This is viable but tight, and requires you to mentally accept that "8 months runway" actually means "5 months of revenue pressure" because the first 3 months won't produce income.

Option 3: Launch on current timeline but with a specific safety net — a part-time arrangement with a friendly ex-employer or fractional CTO/CPO role at one company that covers 30-40% of your baseline costs while the rest of the pipeline builds. This is the "de-risked independent" version. Much less common but also much less fragile.

Your call depends on risk tolerance and what your spouse/partner thinks about the financial pressure.

---

That's the conversation to have this week. Not "are you on board with independent consulting" — you already have that. It's "here's the specific scenario where I launch in September with no signed engagements, here's the burn rate, here's when we'd be under financial pressure, here's what we'd do." If your spouse hasn't seen the specific math, their on-board-ness is for a concept, not for the reality.

The fact that you've kept the runway conversation in your head suggests you might be avoiding the specific version because you're worried it'll make your spouse less supportive. That's a warning sign. Supportive-in-concept but not-aligned-on-specifics is exactly how couples end up fighting about money six months into a new business.

---

Fractional arrangements vary but here's the common shape: you agree to provide 8-15 hours/week as a fractional CPO or senior product advisor at a single company, on a retainer basis (typically $10-20K/month for someone at your experience level). This gives you predictable revenue from day one and doesn't consume your full-time capacity.

The companies that hire fractionals are usually: Series A-B startups that need senior product thinking but can't afford a full-time CPO; post-Series C companies with a specific 6-month initiative that needs a senior PM lead; PE-backed portfolio companies that want senior talent on specific projects.

The best fractional engagements come from your existing network. "Would your company be open to 10 hours/week at $12K/month for the next 6 months, starting in September?" is a very specific ask. Some of those 4-5 network conversations might convert to fractional arrangements even if they won't commit to full project engagements.

The tradeoff: fractional work locks you into a company's cadence, makes it harder to take on larger client engagements, and tends to pay less per hour than project work. But it's the de-risking move for month 1 if you don't have signed project engagements.

---

Here's my honest read. Launching in 6 weeks is viable if you do three things in those 6 weeks: (1) have the full runway conversation with your spouse, (2) go back to your network with the specific fractional ask and see if 1-2 convert, (3) prepare for months 1-3 as business development, not delivery.

If after 4 weeks you have zero fractional commitments and your spouse is not aligned on the 5-months-of-revenue-pressure reality, push back by 2-3 months. That's not failure; that's responding to signal.

Don't push back just because you're nervous. Push back only if the fundamentals haven't come together. The nervousness is always there; it's not good information by itself.

---

You're welcome. The tactics matter, but only if the foundation is real. The foundation is: alignment with your spouse on the actual financial plan, and evidence that your network has at least some commercial intent, not just friendly interest. The launch plan we'd build on that foundation — pricing, positioning, website, legal structure — is the easier part.

Come back in 4 weeks with results from those three things and we can build the actual launch plan then. You'll be building on real information rather than on hopes.
